name: Tomato
water_needs: Regular — 1–2 inches/week; increase during fruiting
pest_notes: Hornworm, aphids, early/late blight. Stake and remove suckers to improve airflow.
care_notes: Harvest when fully colored and yields to gentle pressure. Pick regularly to promote more fruit.
tips: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost (early Feb in Zone 8b); transplant out mid-May when soil hits 60°F.
Bury stems deep when transplanting — roots form all along the buried stem, creating a stronger, drought-resistant plant.
Pinch suckers weekly on indeterminate varieties to direct energy into fruit rather than foliage.
Water deeply and consistently — irregular watering causes blossom-end rot and cracking.
Side-dress with compost or balanced fertilizer once fruits set; switch to low-nitrogen after flowering begins.
safe: ⚠️ Foliage & unripe fruit toxic to cats & dogs (solanine) — ripe fruit generally safe in small amounts (ASPCA).

name: Pepper
water_needs: Moderate — 1 inch/week; consistent moisture prevents blossom drop
pest_notes: Aphids, flea beetles. Susceptible to bacterial spot in wet weather.
care_notes: Harvest green for crunch, or wait for full color change for sweeter flavor. Leave a short stem when cutting.
tips: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost — peppers need the longest indoor head start of any Zone 8b crop.
Don't rush transplanting; soil must be consistently above 65°F or peppers will sulk and stall.
Stake or cage plants before they need it — laden branches snap easily in Willamette Valley wind.
In cool summers, black plastic mulch warms soil and dramatically improves yield.
Leave a short stub of stem when harvesting to reduce disease entry points.
safe: ✅ Non-toxic to cats, dogs & horses — capsaicin is an irritant but not toxic (ASPCA).

name: Peas
water_needs: Moderate — 1 inch/week; avoid overhead watering
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4–6 hrs)
pest_notes: Aphids, powdery mildew in humid weather. Provide trellis support.
care_notes: Harvest Sugar Snap when pods are plump and round; Snow Peas when flat and bright green.
tips: Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked — Feb or early March in Zone 8b. Peas prefer cool, wet conditions.
Inoculate seeds with rhizobium bacteria powder before sowing to maximize nitrogen fixation.
Provide trellis or netting before vines are 4 inches tall; they climb fast and are hard to untangle once sprawling.
Harvest Sugar Snaps daily once pods fill out — picking triggers more production. Missing even one day means tough pods.
Pull plants once temps consistently hit 80°F; sow a fall crop in late August for a second harvest.

name: Lettuce
water_needs: Consistent moisture — shallow roots; bolts without regular watering
sun_needs: Part Shade to Full Sun (4–6 hrs; afternoon shade extends season)
pest_notes: Slugs, aphids, leaf miners. Row cover helps in early spring.
care_notes: Harvest outer leaves or cut whole head at base. Bolt-resistant varieties extend the summer window.
tips: Sow every 2–3 weeks from March through May for continuous harvest — lettuce bolts fast once summer heat arrives.
Plant in part shade behind taller crops in June to extend the season by 3–4 weeks.
Cut-and-come-again: harvest outer leaves only, leaving the center to keep producing.
Scatter seeds thinly and thin to 6–8 inches — crowded lettuce bolts faster and is more prone to disease.
Fall sowing (Aug–Sep) often yields the best heads — cooler temps and less pest pressure.

name: Carrots
water_needs: Deep, infrequent — 1 inch/week encourages deep root growth
soil_pref: Loose, deep, sandy loam, pH 6.0–6.8. Avoid fresh manure — causes forking.
pest_notes: Carrot fly (companion plant with onions to deter). Root knot nematode in heavy soils.
care_notes: Harvest when shoulders are ¾–1 inch diameter. A light frost improves sweetness.
tips: Loosen soil to at least 12 inches — carrots fork and stunt in compacted ground. Raised beds are ideal.
Direct sow only; carrots resent transplanting. Mix seeds with sand for even spacing.
Keep soil consistently moist until germination (10–21 days) — a dry crust stops sprouts dead.
Thin ruthlessly to 2–3 inches once seedlings are 2 inches tall; crowded carrots produce only small fingers.
Leave fall-sown carrots in the ground after frost — cold sweetens them dramatically.

name: Beans
spacing_in: 4–6 inches bush; 6 inches pole (trellis at 6 ft)
water_needs: Moderate — 1 inch/week; drought stress at flowering reduces yield significantly
soil_pref: Well-drained, pH 6.0–6.8. Don't over-fertilize nitrogen — they fix their own.
pest_notes: Mexican bean beetle, aphids, spider mites in hot/dry weather.
care_notes: Pick while pods are firm and snap cleanly. Leaving mature pods on the vine stops production.
tips: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 60°F — beans rot in cold, wet soil.
No fertilizer needed at planting; beans fix their own nitrogen. Too much nitrogen = lush leaves, few pods.
Sow a second batch 3 weeks after the first for a staggered harvest through summer.
Pick beans when pods are finger-length and snap cleanly — let them go and the plant stops producing.
Avoid working around bean plants when foliage is wet to prevent spreading rust and anthracnose.
safe: ✅ Non-toxic to cats, dogs & horses — raw kidney beans are toxic but common garden beans are safe (ASPCA).

name: Cucumbers
water_needs: Consistent moisture — 1–2 inches/week; bitter fruit results from water stress
pest_notes: Cucumber beetles, aphids, powdery mildew. Train up trellis for airflow.
care_notes: Harvest slicing types at 6–8 inches; pickling types at 2–4 inches. Don't let over-ripen on vine.
tips: Start indoors in early April — cucumbers need warm soil, don't rush transplanting before late April/May.
Transplant when soil consistently reaches 65°F in Zone 8b.
Trellis vines to save ground space and improve airflow.
Water at the base; wet foliage encourages powdery mildew.
Feed every 2–3 weeks with balanced organic fertilizer after flowering begins.

name: Onions
water_needs: Regular — 1 inch/week until bulbing; reduce once tops yellow
pest_notes: Onion fly, thrips. Companion plant with carrots.
care_notes: Harvest when tops fall over and yellow. Cure in warm dry location 2–3 weeks before storage.
tips: Start from sets or transplants in Zone 8b — seed-grown onions need a very long season; sets give a reliable head start.
Plant sets 1 inch deep, pointed end up, 4–6 inches apart in early spring.
Stop watering when tops begin to flop and yellow — this signals bulb maturation and prevents rot.
Cure harvested bulbs in a dry, shaded spot with good airflow for 2–3 weeks before storage.
Long-day varieties (like Walla Walla) are best for the Pacific Northwest.
safe: ⚠️ Toxic to cats & dogs (all alliums) — can cause hemolytic anemia even in small amounts (ASPCA).

name: Garlic
water_needs: Moderate — 1 inch/week during spring growth; stop watering when leaves yellow
soil_pref: Well-drained, fertile, pH 6.0–7.0. Add compost at planting.
pest_notes: Onion fly, white rot. Avoid wet soils over winter.
care_notes: Harvest hardneck when 4–6 green leaves remain — each leaf = one papery wrapper. Cure 3–4 weeks in a warm, ventilated spot.
tips: Plant individual cloves in Oct–Nov, pointed end up, 2 inches deep and 6 inches apart.
Mulch heavily after planting to protect from freeze-thaw cycles over winter.
Remove flower scapes (curling green shoots) on hardneck varieties in June — this redirects energy to the bulb.
Stop watering when lower leaves start browning (typically late June). Harvest when 5–6 green leaves remain.
Cure in a dry, shaded, well-ventilated spot for 3–4 weeks. Hardnecks store 6 months; softnecks up to a year.
safe: ⚠️ Toxic to cats & dogs — one of the most toxic alliums by weight; causes oxidative damage to red blood cells (ASPCA).

name: Shallots
water_needs: Moderate — 1 inch/week; reduce as tops yellow
pest_notes: Thrips, white rot. Same care as onions.
care_notes: Harvest when tops fall over. Cure in warm dry location before storage. Each bulb planted produces a cluster of 4–8.
tips: Plant sets in early spring or fall — fall planting gives larger bulbs in Zone 8b.
Space 6 inches apart; each bulb will multiply into a cluster of 4–8.
Like onions, stop watering when tops begin to yellow and fall over.
Lift the whole cluster and separate bulbs before curing.
Excellent keepers — properly cured shallots last 6–8 months in a cool, dry spot.
safe: ⚠️ Toxic to cats & dogs (allium family) — causes hemolytic anemia (ASPCA).

name: Basil
water_needs: Moderate — 1 inch/week; water at base to prevent fungal disease
pest_notes: Aphids, Japanese beetles, fusarium wilt. Pinch flowers to extend harvest.
care_notes: Harvest by pinching stem tips above a leaf pair. Pinch flowers immediately to prevent bolting and bitterness.
tips: Don't plant out until night temps stay above 50°F — basil turns black in the cold.
Pinch flower buds the moment they appear to keep leaves coming for months.
Harvest from the top down, cutting just above a leaf pair to trigger two new shoots.
Water at the base in the morning; wet leaves in afternoon sun causes scorch and fungal issues.
Grow near tomatoes and peppers — it's a good companion and they share the same heat requirements.

name: Marigolds
water_needs: Low to moderate — drought tolerant once established
soil_pref: Average to poor, well-drained. Tolerates low fertility.
pest_notes: Spider mites in drought conditions. Otherwise very pest-resistant.
care_notes: Deadhead spent blooms for continuous flowering. Border vegetable beds with French types for best nematode suppression.
tips: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow in May for summer blooms.
Deadhead spent flowers regularly to extend bloom season through frost.
Plant as a border around vegetable beds — French marigolds (T. patula) are most effective against nematodes.
Allow a few plants to go to seed at season's end to self-sow next year.
African marigolds need staking in windy sites; French types are compact and self-supporting.
safe: ⚠️ Mild GI upset possible in cats & dogs if eaten in quantity — not considered severely toxic (ASPCA).

name: Kale
water_needs: Moderate — 1–1.5 inches/week
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4–6 hrs okay)
pest_notes: Cabbage worms, aphids, flea beetles. Row cover early season is highly effective.
care_notes: Harvest outer leaves first; leave center growing. Flavor improves markedly after a light frost.
tips: Sow indoors Feb–Mar for spring crop; direct sow Jul–Aug for a fall/winter harvest (kale sweetens after frost).
Harvest outer leaves from the bottom up, leaving the growing crown intact.
In Zone 8b, kale often overwinters without protection — harvest through February.
Provide consistent moisture; irregular watering makes leaves tough and bitter.
Succession-plant every 3–4 weeks for a continuous supply rather than a single big planting.
safe: ✅ Non-toxic to cats, dogs & horses — high in oxalates but not considered toxic (ASPCA).

name: Zucchini
water_needs: Deep watering — 1–2 inches/week; avoid wetting foliage to prevent powdery mildew
soil_pref: Rich, well-drained, pH 6.0–7.0. Heavy feeder — amend with compost.
pest_notes: Squash vine borer, aphids, powdery mildew. Check stems weekly for borer entry holes.
care_notes: Harvest at 6–8 inches for best flavor. Overgrown fruit drains the plant — check every 2 days.
tips: Direct sow 2–3 seeds per hill after last frost; thin to the strongest plant.
Check plants daily at peak season — zucchini goes from harvest-size to marrow overnight.
Hand-pollinate if fruit shrivels: use a small brush to transfer pollen from male (thin stem) to female (tiny fruit behind bloom).
Remove old, yellowing leaves to improve airflow and reduce powdery mildew pressure.
One or two plants feeds most families — zucchini is prolific. Three is a gift to neighbors.

name: Squash
spacing_in: 24–48 inches bush; 6–8 ft for vining types
water_needs: Deep, infrequent — 1–2 inches/week
pest_notes: Squash vine borer, cucumber beetles, powdery mildew.
care_notes: Harvest winter squash when rind is hard and can't be scratched with a fingernail. Cure butternut 10 days at 80°F.
tips: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost or direct sow in May when soil is warm.
Winter squash needs a long season — plant early and let vines run; pinch tips in late August to hasten maturation.
Cure harvested squash at room temp for 10–14 days to harden the skin before cold storage.
Leave 2 inches of stem when harvesting — it acts as a natural seal against rot.
In Zone 8b, Butternut and Delicata reliably ripen even in cool summers; Hubbard types need a warm year.

name: Radishes
water_needs: Consistent moisture — drought causes woody, hot-tasting roots
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4–6 hrs)
pest_notes: Flea beetles, cabbage maggot. Fast growth rate reduces vulnerability.
care_notes: Harvest spring types at 1 inch diameter (21–28 days). Don't delay — they split and get pithy fast.
tips: Fastest crop in the garden — from seed to table in 22–30 days. Great for marking slow-germinating rows.
Direct sow every 10–14 days from March through May and again Aug–Sep for continuous harvest.
Don't let radishes stay in the ground too long — they bolt, turn pithy, and get hot.
Thin to 2–3 inches; crowded radishes make no roots.
Daikon and winter radishes (sow Aug) need 60+ days and can stay in the ground through winter in Zone 8b.

name: Beets
spacing_in: 3–4 inches (each seed is a cluster — thin after germination)
water_needs: Regular — 1 inch/week
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4+ hrs)
soil_pref: Loose, deep, pH 6.0–7.0. Avoid compacted soil.
pest_notes: Leaf miners, aphids. Relatively pest-resistant compared to other root veg.
care_notes: Harvest roots at 1.5–3 inches for best texture. Greens are edible at any size — thin seedlings and eat them.
tips: Soak seeds overnight before sowing — each "seed" is actually a cluster of 2–4 seeds, so thinning is essential.
Thin to 3–4 inches once seedlings are 2 inches tall; eat the thinnings as micro-greens.
Both roots and greens are edible — harvest greens early and roots when 2–3 inches in diameter.
Beets tolerate light frost; in Zone 8b, fall-sown beets can be harvested through November.
Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizer — it produces lush tops at the expense of root development.

name: Green Onions
water_needs: Regular — keep consistently moist
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4+ hrs)
pest_notes: Thrips, onion fly. Very low-maintenance.
care_notes: Harvest when ¼ inch diameter. Cut at soil level — they regrow. Succession sow every 3 weeks for continuous supply.
tips: Direct sow thickly from February onward; harvest by pulling whole plants or snipping tops to 2 inches.
Can also grow from saved grocery store green onion bases — stick the root end in soil and it regrows.
Succession-sow every 3 weeks for continuous harvest through fall.
Tolerates light frost well; Zone 8b gardeners can harvest nearly year-round.
Interplant between slower crops like carrots — they repel carrot fly and use space efficiently.
safe: ⚠️ Toxic to cats & dogs (allium family) — causes hemolytic anemia (ASPCA).

name: Spinach
water_needs: Consistent moisture — bolts in drought or heat
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4–6 hrs; afternoon shade extends season)
pest_notes: Leaf miners, aphids, downy mildew.
care_notes: Harvest outer leaves when 3–4 inches. Bolt-resistant varieties extend the spring window. Direct sow again in late summer for fall harvest.
tips: Sow directly as early as Feb in Zone 8b — spinach germinates in cold soil and bolts in heat.
Provide afternoon shade in May to extend harvest; once temps hit 75°F consistently, it's done for spring.
Fall sowing (Aug–Sep) often outperforms spring — cooler temps and fewer pests.
Harvest outer leaves or cut the whole plant 1 inch above soil — it will regrow 1–2 times.
Ensure adequate nitrogen (compost or blood meal) — nitrogen deficiency causes yellowing and small leaves.
safe: ✅ Non-toxic to cats, dogs & horses — high oxalates but not toxic (ASPCA).

name: Cilantro
water_needs: Moderate — 1 inch/week; bolts quickly in heat and drought
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4–6 hrs; afternoon shade in summer)
pest_notes: Aphids, whiteflies. Short-lived by nature — succession-sow every 3 weeks.
care_notes: Harvest leaves before flowering. Let some bolt for coriander seed. Two windows in Zone 8b: Mar–May and Aug–Sep.
tips: Direct sow only — cilantro's taproot resents transplanting.
Sow every 2–3 weeks March through May and again Aug–Sep; it bolts fast in heat.
For seed (coriander), let a late planting go to flower — harvest seed heads when they turn tan.
Thin to 4–6 inches; good airflow slows bolting.
Plant in a spot that gets afternoon shade in Zone 8b summers to squeeze an extra week out of each planting.

name: Cabbage
water_needs: Regular — 1.5 inches/week; inconsistent moisture causes heads to split
pest_notes: Cabbage worms, aphids, clubroot. Rotate brassica beds every 3 years.
care_notes: Harvest when heads are firm and full-sized. Cut at base. Spring types ~80 days; storage types up to 180 days.
tips: Start transplants indoors 6–8 weeks before setting out; harden off for a week before planting.
Space generously — crowded cabbages form small, loose heads.
Use row covers at transplant time to block cabbage maggot flies, which peak in April–May in Zone 8b.
Consistent moisture prevents split heads — mulch heavily to even out soil moisture.
Fall plantings (start Jul, transplant Aug) often produce the best heads in the Pacific Northwest.

name: Cauliflower
water_needs: Regular — 1.5–2 inches/week; critical during head development
pest_notes: Cabbage worms, aphids, clubroot. Rotate brassica beds annually.
care_notes: Blanch white varieties by tying outer leaves over the curd when golf-ball sized. Harvest before curds loosen or yellow.
tips: The most demanding brassica — consistent moisture, fertility, and cool temps are non-negotiable.
Blanch white varieties by tying outer leaves over the developing head when it's the size of a golf ball.
Fall plantings (transplant Aug) almost always outperform spring in Zone 8b.
Cover with row cloth to protect from cabbage worm and aphids, which devastate cauliflower quickly.
Harvest promptly — heads go from perfect to overblown in a few warm days.

name: Strawberries
water_needs: Regular — 1–2 inches/week; drip irrigation preferred to avoid crown rot
pest_notes: Slugs, birds, gray mold (botrytis). Straw mulch reduces slug pressure and keeps fruit clean.
care_notes: Harvest when fully red all over. Keep crowns at soil level when planting. Remove runners first year to focus energy on root establishment.
tips: Plant bare-root crowns in Feb–Apr with the crown at soil level — too deep rots it, too shallow dries it out.
Pinch all flowers the first year on June-bearing types to build root strength for a heavier Year 2 crop.
Renovate beds after harvest: mow foliage to 3 inches, thin runners, and fertilize.
Mulch with straw in Nov to protect crowns from hard freezes.
Replace beds every 3–4 years as yields decline; replant from the strongest runners.

name: Dill
water_needs: Low — drought tolerant; well-drained soil
pest_notes: Aphids, tomato hornworm. Attracts beneficial insects — plant near brassicas.
care_notes: Harvest young feathery fronds anytime. Harvest seed heads when 2/3 of seeds have turned brown. Succession sow every 3 weeks. Do not plant near fennel — they cross-pollinate.
tips: Direct sow only — taproot resents transplanting. Sow from March onward in full sun.
Succession-sow every 3 weeks to maintain a steady supply of fronds.
For pickling dill, let one planting bolt and harvest seed heads when half the seeds turn tan.
Keep away from fennel — they cross-pollinate and produce inferior seed.
Allow one plant to self-sow each year; volunteers appear reliably in the same spot.

name: Parsley
water_needs: Moderate — consistent moisture; tolerates brief dry spells once established
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4–6 hrs)
pest_notes: Parsley worm (swallowtail butterfly larva — relocate, don't kill). Aphids.
care_notes: Harvest outer stems first. Biennial in Zone 8b — overwinters and bolts in year 2. Slow to germinate (3 weeks); soak seeds overnight to speed it up.
tips: Soak seeds 24 hours before sowing — germination is notoriously slow (2–4 weeks) without pre-soaking.
Parsley is biennial; it flowers in Year 2. Treat as an annual and replant each spring.
Harvest outer stems at the base, leaving the center crown to keep producing.
Direct sow outdoors in early March or start indoors in January for transplants.
Tolerates light frost; Zone 8b plants often survive winter and provide early spring harvest.
safe: ✅ Non-toxic in culinary amounts — very large quantities contain furanocoumarins; safe for pets at normal garden exposure (ASPCA).

name: Thyme
water_needs: Low — drought tolerant; overwatering causes root rot
pest_notes: Spider mites in hot/dry conditions. Very few pests overall.
care_notes: Harvest stems before flowering for best flavor. Cut back by ⅓ after flowering to maintain shape. Perennial in Zone 8b.
tips: Buy transplants or start from cuttings — seed-grown thyme is slow and variable in flavor.
Cut back by one-third after flowering to keep plants bushy and prevent woodiness.
Drought-tolerant once established; overwatering is the most common cause of death.
In Zone 8b, thyme is borderline hardy — plant in well-drained soil against a south-facing wall for best survival.
Harvest just before flowering for peak essential oil content.

name: Oregano
water_needs: Low — very drought tolerant; avoid overwatering
pest_notes: Virtually pest-free. Spider mites occasionally in hot conditions.
care_notes: Harvest before flowering for peak flavor. Cut to 3 inches from ground in spring. Perennial in Zone 8b; spreads over time — divide every 2–3 years.
tips: Buy transplants of named varieties (Greek, Italian) — flavor quality varies enormously from seed.
Cut back to 3–4 inches after first flush of growth to encourage bushy habit.
Harvest before and during flowering for strongest flavor.
Oregano is drought-tolerant once established — water deeply but infrequently.
Divide clumps every 2–3 years to rejuvenate; old woody centers lose flavor.

name: Mint
spacing_in: 18–24 inches (grow in containers — spreads aggressively by runners)
water_needs: Moderate to high — prefers consistently moist conditions
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4–6 hrs)
pest_notes: Aphids, spider mites. Generally vigorous and outcompetes most pests.
care_notes: Harvest stems before flowering. Cut to 1 inch from ground to rejuvenate. Strongly recommended to grow in buried containers — rhizomes will take over a bed within a season.
tips: Plant in a buried container (or a pot sunk to its rim) — mint spreads aggressively by underground runners.
Cut back hard to 2–3 inches in midsummer to encourage fresh, tender growth.
Partial shade is fine; mint tolerates more shade than most herbs.
Harvest regularly — frequent cutting keeps mint from going woody.
Spearmint and peppermint are the most useful culinary mints; avoid common "garden mint" which has little flavor.
safe: ⚠️ Mild GI upset possible in cats & dogs if eaten in large amounts — not severely toxic; pennyroyal mint (a different species) IS toxic (ASPCA).

name: Chives
water_needs: Moderate — regular moisture, tolerates some drought
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4+ hrs)
pest_notes: Some thrips. Generally pest-resistant. Edible purple flowers attract pollinators.
care_notes: Snip leaves to 1 inch from soil — they regrow quickly. Perennial; divide clumps every 3 years. Great container herb.
tips: One of the easiest herbs — direct sow or plant divisions in early spring.
Cut leaves to 1–2 inches above soil and the plant regrows repeatedly through the season.
Allow a few flowers to develop — they're edible and beautiful; then deadhead to prevent heavy self-seeding.
Divide clumps every 2–3 years in fall or spring to maintain vigor.
Garlic chives (flat leaves) have a milder garlic flavor — grow them alongside regular chives.
safe: ⚠️ Toxic to cats & dogs (allium family) — even dried chives can cause hemolytic anemia (ASPCA).

name: Rosemary
water_needs: Low — drought tolerant once established; excellent drainage essential
soil_pref: Sandy, well-drained, pH 6.0–8.0. Poor fertility is fine.
pest_notes: Spider mites and aphids in dry conditions. Root rot in wet or clay soils.
care_notes: Harvest stems before flowering. Trim lightly after bloom. Hardy perennial in Zone 8b — provide shelter or mulch in hard freezes.

default.dtm: 2–3 years to full size from seed (buy transplants)

tips: Buy transplants — rosemary from seed is slow and the first year barely establishes.
Plant in the best-draining spot available; rosemary is killed more often by wet roots than by cold.
In Zone 8b, rosemary is mostly hardy but a severe freeze can kill it. Pot up one plant as insurance.
Prune after flowering — never cut back into bare wood as it rarely regrows from there.
Harvest stem tips regularly to keep the plant compact and productive.

name: Sage
water_needs: Low to moderate — drought tolerant; avoid overwatering
pest_notes: Slugs on young plants; spider mites in hot weather. Generally very tough.
care_notes: Harvest leaves before flowering for peak flavor. Cut back by half in spring to prevent woodiness. Perennial in Zone 8b.
tips: Buy transplants of named culinary varieties — decorative sages often lack the flavor of common garden sage.
Cut back by one-third after flowering; remove woody stems every 2 years to rejuvenate.
Excellent in containers — good drainage is essential and pots dry out faster, which sage prefers.
Harvest leaves before flowering for best flavor; young leaves are milder, older ones more intense.
In Zone 8b, sage is reliably hardy but can look ragged in wet winters — cut it back hard in spring.

name: Lavender
water_needs: Low — drought tolerant; excellent drainage is critical (root rot in wet soil)
soil_pref: Sandy, well-drained, pH 6.5–7.5. Poor fertility preferred.
pest_notes: Very few pests. Attracts bees and beneficial insects — great pollinator plant.
care_notes: Harvest flower stems when ½ of flowers are open for best fragrance. Trim by ⅓ after bloom. Do not cut into old wood. Perennial in Zone 8b.

default.dtm: 90 days from seed (buy transplants for best results)

tips: Drainage is everything — lavender dies in wet, heavy soil. Amend with grit or grow in raised beds.
Cut back by one-third immediately after flowering; never cut into bare, brown wood.
In Zone 8b, English lavender (L. angustifolia) is hardiest. Spanish types are tender and need winter protection.
Buy named varieties — flavor and hardiness vary enormously by cultivar.
Harvest flower spikes when about half the buds have opened for the longest-lasting fragrance.
safe: ⚠️ Mild toxicity to cats & dogs — linalool and linalyl acetate can cause nausea and lethargy if ingested in quantity (ASPCA).

name: Broccoli
water_needs: Regular — 1–1.5 inches/week
pest_notes: Cabbage worms, aphids, clubroot, flea beetles. Row cover early season is highly effective.
care_notes: Harvest central head when tight, dark green, and before any flowers open. Side shoots follow — keep cutting for weeks of harvest.
tips: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; set out in early April in Zone 8b for summer crop.
Space 18–24 inches apart — crowded plants produce only small, loose side shoots.
Harvest the central head while florets are still tightly closed; once yellow flowers open, flavor drops fast.
After removing the central head, side shoots continue producing for weeks — keep harvesting regularly.
Use row cover from transplant through June to block imported cabbageworm, which devastates broccoli in Zone 8b.
safe: ✅ Non-toxic to cats, dogs & horses — GI upset possible in large amounts but not toxic (ASPCA).

name: Brussels Sprouts
water_needs: Regular — 1–1.5 inches/week
soil_pref: Fertile, well-drained, pH 6.0–7.0. Add lime if pH is below 6.5.
pest_notes: Cabbage worms, aphids, clubroot. Longest growing brassica — protect early with row cover.
care_notes: Harvest sprouts from bottom up when firm and marble-sized. Remove yellowing leaves. Flavor improves after frost. Pinch the growing tip in August to speed maturation.
tips: Start seeds in June for a Nov–Feb harvest — Brussels sprouts need a long season and improve with frost.
Remove lower yellowing leaves as the season progresses to direct energy upward.
Pinch the growing tip in mid-September to help all sprouts mature simultaneously.
Stake plants in fall — tall stems become top-heavy and blow over in Willamette Valley storms.
Harvest from the bottom up when sprouts are 1–1.5 inches and tight. Loose, open sprouts are overdue.
safe: ✅ Non-toxic to cats, dogs & horses — GI upset in large amounts (ASPCA).

name: Bok Choy
water_needs: Regular — keep consistently moist; bolts in drought or heat
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4–6 hrs)
pest_notes: Aphids, flea beetles, slugs. Same pest pressures as cabbage.
care_notes: Harvest baby bok choy at 4–6 inches tall, or full heads at 12–14 inches. Cut at base.
tips: Direct sow or transplant in early spring and again in late summer — bolts quickly in long days and heat.
Baby bok choy varieties (45 days) are much easier than full-size types in Zone 8b's variable springs.
Harvest whole plants by cutting at the base, or harvest outer leaves individually.
Use row cover against flea beetles, which riddle leaves with holes in spring.
Fall crops (start Aug, harvest Oct–Nov) are often superior — less pest pressure and slower bolting.

name: Arugula
water_needs: Moderate — consistent moisture; bolts quickly in heat
sun_needs: Part Shade to Full Sun (4–6 hrs; shade cloth extends summer season)
pest_notes: Flea beetles (row cover helps). Fast growth means a short pest vulnerability window.
care_notes: Harvest outer leaves at 3–4 inches. Younger leaves are milder; larger leaves are peppery. Succession sow every 2 weeks in spring and fall.
tips: Direct sow as early as March — arugula germinates readily in cool soil.
Harvest young leaves (3–4 inches) for mild flavor; older leaves develop intense, peppery heat.
Succession-sow every 2–3 weeks March through May and Aug–Sep. It bolts fast in summer heat.
Thin to 4–5 inches; crowded plants bolt faster and have less airflow.
Allow one late-summer plant to flower and set seed — arugula self-sows reliably and volunteers appear every spring.

name: Kohlrabi
water_needs: Regular — 1 inch/week; consistent moisture prevents cracking
pest_notes: Cabbage worms, flea beetles.
care_notes: Harvest when bulb is 2–3 inches diameter. Larger sizes become woody and tough. Both spring and fall crops work well in Zone 8b.
tips: Start indoors 4–5 weeks before transplanting, or direct sow in April and again in July.
Harvest when bulbs are 2–3 inches in diameter — larger kohlrabi gets woody and loses sweetness.
Peel before eating; skin on large kohlrabi is fibrous even after cooking.
Use row cover to prevent cabbage maggot damage, especially in spring.
Interplant with lettuce — kohlrabi uses vertical space and shades lettuce roots in warm spells.

name: Parsnips
water_needs: Deep, infrequent — 1 inch/week; like carrots
soil_pref: Deep, loose, well-drained, pH 6.0–7.0. Avoid fresh manure.
pest_notes: Carrot fly, parsnip canker. Slow to germinate — mark rows clearly.
care_notes: Can leave in ground through winter — frost converts starch to sugar, dramatically improving flavor. Harvest before ground freezes hard or dig in early spring.
tips: Sow fresh seed in April — parsnip seed loses viability rapidly. Buy new seed each year.
Germination is slow (3–4 weeks); keep soil consistently moist and mark the row with fast-germinating radishes.
Thin to 4–6 inches for full-size roots; parsnips rarely fork in loose, deep soil.
Leave roots in the ground until after several frosts — cold converts starches to sugar for superb flavor.
Harvest through winter in Zone 8b; mulch the row to keep soil workable during hard freezes.
safe: ✅ Non-toxic to cats, dogs & horses — wild parsnip sap causes skin irritation in sunlight but garden parsnips are safe (ASPCA).

name: Turnips
water_needs: Regular — 1 inch/week; drought causes bitter, cracked roots
pest_notes: Flea beetles, cabbage root fly.
care_notes: Harvest spring types at 2–3 inches diameter — they get pithy when large. Harvest greens at any size. Fall types are more forgiving of size.
tips: Direct sow in April for summer crop; sow again in late July for a fall/winter harvest.
Harvest young, golf-ball-sized turnips for sweetest flavor; over-mature roots get pithy and sharp.
Turnip greens are edible and nutritious — harvest outer leaves as the root develops.
Fall sowings tolerate hard frost; roots sweeten after cold exposure just like parsnips.
Thin to 4–5 inches — crowded plants produce only small, stressed roots.

name: Fennel
spacing_in: 12 inches bulb types; 18 inches herb types
water_needs: Moderate — 1–2 inches/week; tolerates dry spells once established
pest_notes: Aphids, parsley worm. Important: allelopathic — plant away from most vegetables.
care_notes: Harvest bulb when tennis ball sized. Harvest fronds anytime. Collect seeds when turning brown. Keep isolated from tomatoes, peppers, and beans — stunts their growth.
tips: Plant fennel away from most vegetables — it's allelopathic and stunts growth of tomatoes, peppers, and beans nearby.
For bulbing (Florence) fennel, direct sow in July for a fall harvest — spring-sown plants bolt before bulbing.
Hill soil up around the developing bulb (blanching) for a sweeter, more tender result.
Harvest bulbs when they are tennis-ball to softball size; larger ones get stringy.
Herb fennel can stay in the garden as a perennial in Zone 8b — its flowers attract beneficial insects.

name: Celeriac
water_needs: High — regular deep watering 1.5–2 inches/week; mulch to retain moisture
soil_pref: Rich, moist, fertile, pH 6.0–7.0. Heavily amend with compost.
pest_notes: Aphids, celery leaf tier, leaf blight.
care_notes: Harvest when 3–4 inches diameter. Flavor improves with light frost. Trim outer stalks as the plant grows to direct energy to the bulb. Very long season — start early.
tips: Start seeds indoors in late January — celeriac needs the longest indoor period of any vegetable.
Transplant out in May; don't rush — it's cold-sensitive when young.
Keep consistently moist; drought stress causes cracked, pithy roots.
Remove side shoots and lower leaves through summer to expose and size up the root crown.
Harvest after first frosts sweeten the roots; stores in root cellar or fridge for months.

name: Leeks
water_needs: Regular — 1 inch/week; consistent moisture needed for tender shanks
pest_notes: Onion fly, thrips, white rot. Rotate allium beds annually.
care_notes: Hill soil around stalks as they grow to blanch (whiten) the shank. Harvest at 1 inch diameter or larger. Cold-hardy — can leave in ground into winter in Zone 8b.
tips: Start indoors in January — sow thickly in a flat and thin to pencil-width before transplanting.
Transplant into deep holes (6–8 inches), dropping the seedling in without backfilling — soil settles around it, blanching the stem naturally.
Hill soil up around stems through the season for longer white shanks.
Leeks tolerate hard frost; in Zone 8b they can often be harvested through January.
King Richard types mature faster (summer); American Flag types are hardier (fall/winter harvest).
safe: ⚠️ Toxic to cats & dogs (allium family) — causes hemolytic anemia (ASPCA).

name: Swiss Chard
water_needs: Regular — 1 inch/week; tolerates some drought once established
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (4–6 hrs)
pest_notes: Leaf miners, aphids, slugs. Generally tough and productive.
care_notes: Harvest outer leaves when 8–10 inches. Cut and come again — center keeps producing. Tolerates light frost and is productive from spring through fall in Zone 8b.
tips: Direct sow from March onward; chard tolerates both light frost and summer heat better than most greens.
Each "seed" is a cluster — thin to 6 inches and eat the thinnings.
Harvest outer leaves from the bottom, leaving the center growing point intact for continuous production.
In Zone 8b, chard planted in spring often produces all the way to November or beyond.
Rainbow chard varieties (Bright Lights) are equally flavorful but more ornamental in the garden.
safe: ✅ Non-toxic to cats, dogs & horses — high oxalates but not considered toxic (ASPCA).

name: Celery
water_needs: High — consistently moist 1.5–2 inches/week. Most water-demanding vegetable in the garden.
sun_needs: Full Sun to Part Shade (6+ hrs)
soil_pref: Rich, moisture-retentive, pH 6.0–7.0. Heavily amend with compost.
pest_notes: Aphids, celery leaf tier, early blight.
care_notes: Harvest outer stalks as needed or cut entire plant at base. Mound soil around stalks to blanch for milder flavor. Slow to mature — start very early indoors.
tips: Start indoors in late January — celery needs 10–12 weeks before transplanting and is slow to germinate.
Surface-sow seeds; they need light to germinate — do not cover.
Consistent, abundant moisture is critical; drought stress causes stringy, hollow stalks.
Blanch stalks by wrapping newspaper or cardboard around the plant two weeks before harvest for milder flavor.
Celery is the most demanding vegetable in the garden — a rich, moisture-retentive soil is non-negotiable.
safe: ✅ Non-toxic to cats, dogs & horses — seeds and leaves are safe at normal culinary exposure (ASPCA).

name: Pumpkin
water_needs: Deep watering — 1–2 inches/week; critical during fruit set
soil_pref: Rich, well-drained, pH 6.0–6.8. Heavy feeder — amend deeply with compost.
pest_notes: Squash vine borer, cucumber beetles, powdery mildew. Check stems for borer entry holes.
care_notes: Harvest when rind is hard, stem is dry and corky, and color is fully developed. Cure at 80°F for 10 days. Always leave 3–4 inches of stem — don't use the stem as a carrying handle.
tips: Direct sow 2–3 seeds per hill in late May when soil is warm; thin to the strongest plant.
Allow 6–8 feet per plant minimum — pumpkin vines are large and will take over a bed.
Pollinate by hand in cool or rainy weather by brushing a male flower (thin stem) against a female (tiny fruit behind bloom).
Leave 2 inches of stem when cutting — a short stub dramatically slows rot in storage.
Cure harvested pumpkins at 80–85°F for 10 days to harden the skin; then store in a cool, dry spot.
safe: ✅ Non-toxic to cats, dogs & horses — plain pumpkin is actually beneficial for pet digestion (ASPCA).
